gebruik.e: From Middle Dutch gebruuc. Equivalent to a deverbal from gebruiken.

grap.e: Related to grijpen. First attested in the 18th century.

grens.e: Borrowed in the 16th century from Middle Low German grense, grenitse and/or German Grenze, both ultimately from Old Polish granica, from Proto-Slavic *granica.

s.def: (notably the diminutive, with the indefinite article: een geurtje) An appearance, something associated, especially in a negative sense
examples: Ik betrouw dat zaakje niet, er zit een geurtje aan!

geur.e: From Middle Dutch gore. Related to Dutch goor.

genot.e: From Middle Dutch ghenot, from Old Dutch *ginot, from Proto-Germanic *ganutą. Related to genieten.

examples:

groep.e: Probably borrowed from Italian gruppo, though possibly borrowed via French groupe. Doublet of krop.

geluid.e: From Middle Dutch geluut. Equivalent to ge- + luiden (“to sound, resound”).

geloof.e: From earlier gelove, from Middle Dutch gelôve, from Old Dutch gilōvo, from Proto-Germanic *galaubô.

gif.e:
From Middle Dutch gift, earlier also gicht, from Old Dutch *gift, from Proto-West Germanic *gifti, from Proto-Germanic *giftiz.
The word gift derives from the same source, and the words were not formally distinguished until early modern Dutch.

examples:
early 16th century, Evangelien vanden spinrocke metter glosen bescreuen ter eeren vanden vrouwen, "chapter" (paragraph) 11, 1910 edition, page 39, "gloss".

grootmoeder.e: From groot- + moeder, calque of Middle French [Term?].

gehoorzamen.e: From Middle Dutch gehoorsamen, from Old Dutch *gihōrsamon. Equivalent to gehoorzaam + -en.

groeien.e: From Middle Dutch groeyen, from Old Dutch gruoien, from Proto-Germanic *grōaną, from Proto-Indo-European *gʰreh₁- (“to grow, become green”). Cognate with English grow.

examples: Ik voel me goed.
Het is een goede informatiebron.

Hij speelt goed tennis.

goed.e: From Middle Dutch goet, from Old Dutch guot, from Proto-West Germanic *gōd, from Proto-Germanic *gōdaz, from Proto-Indo-European *gʰedʰ-.

gezond.e: From Middle Dutch gesont, from Old Dutch gisunt, from Proto-West Germanic *gasund, cognate with German gesund, Old English ġesund, and Old High German gisunt.

examples: De zonsondergang kleurde de lucht in prachtige gele tinten.
Ze droeg een elegant jurkje met een gele bloemenprint.

geel.e: From Middle Dutch gelu, from Old Dutch *gelo, from Proto-West Germanic *gelu, from Proto-Germanic *gelwaz.

s.def: enough, to enough of a degree, with sufficient frequency
examples: Is de auto groot genoeg voor drie kinderen?

genoeg.e: From Middle Dutch genuch, from Old Dutch *ginuog, from Proto-Germanic *ganōgaz. Cognate with West Frisian genôch, German genug, English enough, Low German noog, Swedish nog.

gemeen.e: From Middle Dutch gemêen, from Old Dutch *gimēni, from Proto-West Germanic *gamainī, from Proto-Germanic *gamainiz.

gisteren.e: From Middle Dutch gisteren, from Old Dutch *gestaron, from Proto-Germanic *gesteran.

examples: De lucht is vandaag grijs.
Hij heeft grijs haar.

grijs.e: From Middle Dutch grijs, from Old Dutch *grīs, from Proto-West Germanic *grīs, from Proto-Germanic *grīsaz.

gerecht.e:
From Middle Dutch gerichte, gerechte (“court of law”), from Old Dutch *gerihte, from Proto-Germanic *garihtiją (“something prepared or arranged”). Compare Old English gerihte. The sense "dish" developed independently already in Old Dutch gerihte (“meal”).
Cognate in both meanings: German Gericht.

s.def: past participle of baren (“give birth”); born
examples: Ik ben geboren in 1977.

gemak.e: From Middle Dutch gemac, from Old Dutch *gimak, from Proto-Germanic *gamaką, a substantivised form of *gamakaz.

examples: op de grond

grond.e: From Middle Dutch gront, from Old Dutch grunt, from Proto-West Germanic *grundu, from Proto-Germanic *grunduz.

examples: In sommige culturen is het gebruikelijker om thee te drinken dan koffie.
Het schudden van handen is een gebruikelijke begroeting in veel landen.

gebruikelijk.e: From Middle Dutch gebrukelijc, from Middle High German gebrûchlich (whence German gebräuchlich). Equivalent to gebruiken + -e- + -lijk.

examples: Dit is maar een gewone postzegel.
Hij is een gewone jongen.

Je moet gewoon op het knopje drukken.

gewoon.e: From Middle Dutch gewone. Equivalent to ge- + wennen with ablaut.

examples: De textuur van de stenen vloer was grof.

grof.e: From Middle Dutch grof, from Old Dutch *grof, from Proto-West Germanic *grob, from Proto-Germanic *grubaz.

examples: Iedereen wordt in gelijke gevallen gelijk behandeld.

landen gelijk Engeland en Frankrijk

Etymology tree
Old Dutch gilīk
Middle Dutch gelijc
Dutch gelijk
Inherited from Middle Dutch gelijc, from Old Dutch gilīk.

geen.e: From Middle Dutch negeen, ne gene, (also as engheen, en geen), from Old Dutch chein, ghein, nehein (“not one; none”), ultimately corresponding to Proto-Germanic *nehw ainaz (“nor one”). Compare German kein, Yiddish קיין (keyn), English none.

examples: De wond heeft tijd nodig om te genezen.
De arts doet haar best om de patient te genezen.

genezen.e: From Middle Dutch genesen, from Old Dutch *ginesan, from Proto-Germanic *ganesaną, in which the Proto-Germanic *ga- prefix indicates completeness (perfectivity), and the Proto-Indo-European *nes- (“to return home”) recovery. The now-obsolete verb generen (“to provide for oneself”) is also derived...

gracht.e: From Middle Dutch gracht, from Old Dutch *graft, *graht, from Proto-Germanic *graftuz. Equivalent to graven (“to dig”) + -t (“verbal noun”).

Een gemeenzame uitdrukking.

grijsaard.e: Derived from Middle Dutch grisaert. Equivalent to grijs (“grey”) + -aard, although grijs may be associated with the Middle Dutch verb grisen (“to mutter, grunt”).

gerucht.e: From Middle Dutch geruchte. By surface analysis, ge- + rucht.

griekenland.e: From Middle Dutch Griekenlant, from Old Dutch Grieklant. Equivalent to Griek (“Greek, Greek person”) + -en- + land (“land, country”).

gips.e: Ultimately from Latin gypsum, from Ancient Greek γύψος (gúpsos), probably from a Semitic language.

gij.e:
From Middle Dutch gī, ghi, from Old Dutch gī, from Proto-West Germanic *jiʀ, from Proto-Germanic *jīz, Northwest Germanic variant of *jūz, from Proto-Indo-European *yúHs. Doublet of jij.

gemet.e: From Middle Dutch ghemet, from Old Dutch [Term?], from Proto-West Germanic *gametan, equivalent to ge- + meten. Cognates include Old English ġemet, Old Saxon gimet, Old High German gimez.

gieten.e: From Middle Dutch gieten, from Old Dutch gietan, from Proto-West Germanic *geutan, from Proto-Germanic *geutaną, from Proto-Indo-European *ǵʰewd- (“to pour”).

groeten.e: From Middle Dutch groeten, from Old Dutch *gruoten, from Proto-West Germanic *grōtijan, from Proto-Germanic *grōtijaną.

graver.e: From Middle Dutch grāvere. Equivalent to graven + -er.

examples: Ik loop graag op het strand.

geraamte.e: From Middle Dutch geraemte. Equivalent to ge- + raam (“frame”) + -te.

geste.e: From Middle Dutch, from Medieval Latin gestura.

gladiolus.e: Borrowed from Latin gladiolus, a diminutive form of gladius (“sword”).

gade.e:
From Middle Dutch gade, from gegade, from Old Dutch *gigado. Substantivised form of the past participle of gaden, which is now obsolete.
Related to eega, gading, gader, tegader, gaderen, vergaderen, gegadigde, allegaartje, weerga and possibly also goed. Cognate with German Gatte.

gauw.e: From Middle Dutch gouwe, gauwe (“quick, fast, hasty”), alteration of gâ, from Old Dutch gā, from Proto-Germanic *ganhuz, *ganhwaz (“sudden, quick”), of unknown origin. Cognate with Low German gau (“quick, fast”), German jäh (“sudden, abrupt”). More at gay.

golgotha.e: Borrowed from Latin Golgotha, from Ancient Greek Γολγοθᾶ (Golgothâ), from Aramaic גּלגּלת.

geweld.e: From Middle Dutch gewelt, influenced by umlauted geweldich (modern geweldig), from Old Dutch giwalt, ultimately pertaining to Proto-West Germanic *ga- and *waldōn (“to govern, rule”). Cognate with English wald, German Gewalt.

gesprek.e:
From Middle Dutch gesprec (“speaking, speech”), from Old Dutch *gisprekki, from Proto-Germanic *gasprakją. Equivalent to ge- + spreken. Its use as a term for “conversation” dates to early Modern Dutch, in this sense it ultimately displaced older gespraak.
Compare Low German Gespreek, German Gespr...

gesloten.e: Past participle of sluiten (“to close, shut”).

gevecht.e: From Middle Dutch gevecht, from Old Dutch [Term?], from Proto-West Germanic [Term?]. By surface analysis, ge- + vechten. Cognate to Old High German gifeht, Middle High German gevechte, German Gefecht, Old English gefeoht and West Frisian gefjocht.

godverdomme.e: Univerbation of God + verdomme. Literally, “God dammit, may God damn”. The -me is not the pronoun me, but represents the dated subjunctive suffix -e (preceded by a second -m- to mark the penultimate syllable as closed and the pronunciation of its vowel as short).

gebieden.e: From Middle Dutch gebieden, from Old Dutch gibiedan. Equivalent to ge- + bieden.

gedicht.e: From Middle Dutch gedichte. Equivalent to an action noun derived from dichten, from Latin dictare. Compare German Gedicht, Old English gediht.

geest.e:
From Middle Dutch gêest, from Old Dutch gēst, from Proto-West Germanic *gaist, from Proto-Germanic *gaistaz, from Proto-Indo-European *ǵʰeysd-, *ǵʰisd- (“anger, agitation”).

gooien.e: From Middle Dutch goyen, further etymology debated. Possibly from an ancestor of gauw (“quickly, soon”), although other origins have been suggested.

geeuwen.e: From Middle Dutch geeuwen, geuwen, ghewen, gewen, from Old Dutch *gewon, *giwon, from Proto-West Germanic *giwōn, from Proto-Germanic *giwōną.

goot.e: From Middle Dutch gōte, from Old Dutch *gota, from Proto-Germanic *gutō. Cognate with German Gosse.

gordeldier.e: From gordel (“belt”) + dier (“animal”), calque of German Gürteltier. First attested in the 1790s.

s.def: Gobelin, a rich, textured type of hand-stitched tapestry, fit to decorate a wall
a similar industrial fabric, used to cover furniture etc.

gobelin.e: From the name of Parisian manufacturer Jean Gobelin.

s.def: hamlet; a tiny settlement, often without a church
examples:
1637 January 19, Pieter Corneliszoon Hooft, "861 Aen Zijne Hooghejt", in H.W van Tricht (ed.), De briefwisseling van P.C. Hooft, vol. 2, Tjeenk Willink (publ., 1977), page 914, lines 5 to 8.
[…] De Bijlmer is een gehucht oft gebujrte behoorende, als een lidt van Weesperkerspel, onder 't Castelajnschap van Mujden ende Baljuwschap van Goejlandt, ende paelende aen zeker Meerken genaemt de Bijlmermeer.

gehucht.e: From Middle Dutch ghehochte, ghehuchte, from Old Dutch *gihufti. Ultimately a combination of ge- + hof + -te. Compare German Gehöft, which is perhaps of Dutch origin.

s.def: glory, great beauty, renown or splendour
geography (study of physical structure and inhabitants of the Earth)

geografie.e: Borrowed from French géographie, from Latin geōgraphia, from Ancient Greek γεωγραφία (geōgraphía).

s.def: geology (academic discipline studying the structure of the earth or other planets)

geologie.e: Borrowed from French géologie or English geology, from Latin geōlogia. Equivalent to geo- + -logie.

examples: De gebouwen van een stad.

grijpen.e: From Middle Dutch gripen, from Old Dutch grīpan, from Proto-West Germanic *grīpan, from Proto-Germanic *grīpaną, from Proto-Indo-European *gʰreyb- (“to grasp, grab”).

s.def: cool (in the sense of nice or impressive)
examples: Skateboarden is graver dan inline-skaten! — Skateboarding is cooler than inline skating!

s.def: a hamlet in Lopik, Utrecht, Netherlands

graaf.e: From Middle Dutch grâve, from Old Dutch grāvo, from Proto-West Germanic *garāfijō.

s.def: a village in Groningen, Groningen, Netherlands

glimmen.e: From Middle Dutch glimmen, from Old Dutch *glimman, from Proto-Germanic *glimmaną, from Proto-Indo-European *ǵʰley- (“to gleam, shimmer, glow”). See also Norwegian glim, dialectal Old Swedish glim, glimma.

examples: Bovendien maakt thans òf de ongeloovige geest des predikants, òf die van het echtpaar, òf van het gehoor, òf zelfs van allen, die bij het sluiten van den echt betrokken zijn, dat geheele gebed en al dat gezegen dikwerf tot eene zoetsappige huichelarij.

gouverneur.e: From Middle Dutch gouverneur, borrowed from Middle French gouverneur.

garen.e: From Middle Dutch garen, from Old Dutch *garn, from Proto-West Germanic *garn.

s.def: monstrosity, monster, something or someone very ugly
examples: Dat gebouw is een gedrocht.

gedrocht.e: From Middle Dutch gedrochte, ghedrochte (“apparition, vision”).

gewoonte.e: From Middle Dutch gewoonte. Equivalent to gewoon + -te.

examples: Gezien zijn ouderdom is deze behandeling geen goed idee.

geweldig.e: From Middle Dutch geweldich, from Old Dutch *giweldig, composed of giwald + -ig. The base word descends from Proto-Germanic *gawaldiz. By surface analysis, geweld + -ig.

gevallen.e: From ge- + vallen. Cognate with German gefallen (“to please, appeal to”).

s.def: one of the three regions constituting the equivalent of a state in the federal structure of Belgium

gewest.e: Possibly from the circumfix ge- -te with the root of the verb wezen (in one of its old senses, "to inhabit").

examples: Deze technologie is de meest gangbare in moderne smartphones.
De gangbare mening is dat het project succesvol zal zijn.
Is dit ticket nog gangbaar voor de trein?

Deze smartphone is momenteel zeer gangbaar op de markt.
Biologische producten zijn steeds gangbaarder geworden.

gravin.e: From Middle Dutch grâvinne. Equivalent to graaf + -in.

geil.e:
From Middle Dutch gheil, from Old Dutch *geil, from Proto-West Germanic *gail, from Proto-Germanic *gailaz, from Proto-Indo-European *gʰoylos (“frothing, tempestuous, wanton”). Cognate to English gole, German geil.
The slang meaning of "cool" was likely borrowed from German geil.

geding.e: From Middle Dutch gedinge. Equivalent to ge- + dingen (“to plead”).

getuigenis.e: From Middle Dutch getugenisse, from getugen (“to bear witness”) + -nisse (modern getuigen + -nis). Compare German Zeugnis.

s.def: to testify, bear witness, give testimony, especially in court
examples: Getuigen van Jehova kloppen overal aan om te getuigen van Gods woord.

s.def: to constitute an indication or evidence

getuigen.e: From Middle Dutch getugen, from Old Dutch *gitiugon. Equivalent to getuige + -en.

geometrie.e: From Middle Dutch geometrie, from Old French geometrie, from Latin geōmetria, from Ancient Greek γεωμετρία (geōmetría).

s.def: Gouda (a city and municipality of South Holland, Netherlands)

gouda.e: First attested as golda in 1143. Derived from the hydronym Gouwe, derived in turn from a compound of Old Dutch gold (“gold”) and ā (“watercourse, river”).

s.def: a witness who testifies in a case
as shown by, judging by, witness

getuige.e:
From Middle Dutch getuge, from the verb getugen (modern getuigen).
Cognate with German Zeuge.

gloeien.e: Inherited from Middle Dutch gloeyen, inherited from Old Dutch *gluoien, inherited from Proto-West Germanic *glōan, inherited from Proto-Germanic *glōaną.

s.def: a male given name derived from Gerard

gert.e: Shortening of Gerard, from Middle Dutch gerart, from older gerhart.

a supporter of the insurrectionist faction in the Spanish Netherlands, who became the enemies of the Spanish crown, formalised in 1566 (originally a polemic insult, but soon reappropriated)
any one of the Protestants in the Low Countries, in particular of a Reformed tradition, especially as opposed to Catholics
belonging or related to the faction of the geuzen (sense 1 above)

geus.e: From Middle French gueux (“beggar”), from Middle Dutch guyte (“rascal, good-for-nothing, freeloader”). Doublet of guit.

grauw.e: From Middle Dutch grau, from Old Dutch grāo, from Proto-West Germanic *grāu, from Proto-Germanic *grēwaz.

generatie.e: From Middle French generacion, and their source, Latin generātiō, from generāre (“to beget, generate”).

gelden.e: From Middle Dutch gelden, from Old Dutch *geldan, from Proto-West Germanic *geldan, from Proto-Germanic *geldaną, from Proto-Indo-European *gʰeldʰ- (“to pay”).

gedurende.e: From Middle Dutch gedurende, at first used only as a postposed adverb. Originally the present participle of geduren (“to continue, to endure”), which is now archaic.

examples: De derde maandag in januari is in de VS de Martin Luther Kingdag, een nationale feestdag, gewijd aan King en zijn gedachtegoed.

gelijkenis.e: From Middle Dutch gelîkenisse, from Old Dutch gilīcnussi, from Proto-West Germanic *galīkanassī (“likeness”). Equivalent to gelijken + -nis.

examples: De chemische structuur van DNA, een dubbele helix, werd in 1953 ontdekt door Rosalind Franklin, die gelijktijdig met James D. Watson en Francis Crick aan het onderzoek werkte.

gelijktijdig.e:
From gelijk + tijd + -ig.
Compare German gleichzeitig.

s.def: to gush, (especially precipitation) pour down, flow strongly irregularly
examples: Het regenwater gutste van de daken.

gunst.e:
From Middle Dutch gunst, gonst, prefixed from ge- + onst, onste, the latter from Old Dutch anst, from Proto-West Germanic *unsti, from Proto-Germanic *unstiz.
Equivalent to gunnen + -st.

gnosticisme.e: From Ancient Greek γνωστικός (gnōstikós, “relating to knowledge”) + -isme.

gesteente.e: From steen + ge- -te. Compare German Gestein.

gezag.e: From Middle Dutch gesach. Equivalent to ge- + zeggen.

gevangen.e: From Proto-West Germanic *gafangan, past participle of *fą̄han, equivalent to ge- + vangen. Comparable to Middle Low German gevangen.

gevangene.e: Declined form of gevangen, the past participle of vangen. Compare German Gefangener and Gefangene.

gelopen.e: Inherited from Middle Dutch gelôpen. Equivalent to ge- + lopen.

gideon.e: Ultimately from Biblical Hebrew גִּדְעוֹן (“smasher, feller”), perhaps influenced by Latin Gedeon, from Ancient Greek Γεδεων (Gedeōn).

gelieven.e: From Middle Dutch gelieven, from Old Dutch gelievon. Equivalent to ge- + lieven.

gezelschap.e: From Middle Dutch geselschap, from Old Dutch *giselliskap. Equivalent to gezel + -schap. Cognate with German Gesellschaft.

gezel.e: From Middle Dutch geselle, from Old Dutch gisello, from Proto-Germanic *gasaljô.

From Middle Dutch heerse, from Proto-Germanic *hirsijô, potentially derived in turn from an unknown substrate language or, alternatively, from Proto-Indo-European *ker- (“feed, nourish”).

gaard.e:
From Middle Dutch gaert, from Old Dutch *gart, from Proto-West Germanic *gard, from Proto-Germanic *gardaz, from Proto-Indo-European *gʰórdʰos or *gʰerdʰ-.
Cognate with English yard (compare also garden), obsolete German Gart, German Garten, Danish gård.

gotspe.e:
From the Bargoens register, borrowed from Yiddish חוצפה (chotspe), from Hebrew חֻצְפָּה (ḥuṣpā), from Aramaic חוצפא.
Cognate with English chutzpah and German Chuzpe.

gemeente.e: From Middle Dutch gemeende, from Old Dutch *gimēnitha, from Proto-West Germanic *gamainī + *-iþu. Corresponds to gemeen (“common”) + -te.

gaar.e: From Middle Dutch gaer (“ready”), from Old Dutch garo (“ready”), from Proto-West Germanic *garu, from Proto-Germanic *garwaz (“ready”). More at yare.

gaaf.e: From Middle Dutch gāve, from Proto-Germanic *gēbiz, cognate with Old Norse gæfr (“comfortable”), Danish gæv. Derived from the verb *gebaną (“to give”).

geschrift.e: From Middle Dutch geschrift, from Old Dutch giscrift. By surface analysis, ge- + schrift.

gewoonlijk.e: From Middle Dutch gewoonlike. Equivalent to gewoon + -lijk. Cf. German gewöhnlich.

From Middle Dutch geck (“madman”). Earlier origin uncertain, but perhaps from an imitative verb also found in Swedish gäcka (“to mock”).

grotendeels.e: Univerbation of groten + deels; compare German größtenteils.

gletsjer.e: Borrowed from German Gletscher. First attested in the late 17th c. in the form gleser, then the spelling shifted to gletscher in the 18th c.

godin.e: From Middle Dutch godinne, from Old Dutch *gudinna, *gudin, from Proto-West Germanic *gudini, from Proto-Germanic *gudinī (“goddess”); cognates include Old English gyden, Old High German gutin (German Göttin). By surface analysis, god + -in.

genoegen.e: From Middle Dutch genoegen, the gerund of the verb genoegen (“to have enough, to be satisfied”).

gestreng.e: From Middle Dutch gestrenge, possibly borrowed from Middle High German gestrenge. By surface analysis, ge- + streng.

First attested as gole in 1298. Derived from the hydronym Geul.
See also Limburgish Gäöl.

s.def: a village in Meerssen, Limburg, Netherlands

geulle aan de maas.e:
Derived from the hydronym Geul. The identifier aan de Maas ("on the Meuse") was added at a later date to distinguish the settlement from Geulle.
See also Limburgish Gäöl aan de Maas.

s.def: a hamlet in Meerssen, Limburg, Netherlands
a village and former municipality of Leudal, Limburg, Netherlands

grathem.e:
First attested as grathem in 1116. Perhaps a compound of either Old Dutch grat (“hill ridge, elevated place”) or *grat (“pinewood branch”) and heem (“home, settlement”).
See also Limburgish Gratem.

s.def: a hamlet in Leudal, Limburg, Netherlands

gendijk.e:
First attested as Dijkerhof in 1869. Compound of the dative singular version of the definite article ge and dijk (“levee”).
See also Limburgish Gendiek.

s.def: a hamlet in Leudal, Limburg, Netherlands

geneijgen.e:
First attested as Geneygen in 1803-1820. Compound of the dative singular form of the definite article ge and eigen (“landed property”).
See also Limburgish Geneige.

s.def: a hamlet in Leudal, Limburg, Netherlands

gerhegge.e:
First attested as Gerheggen in 1803-1820. Compound of the feminine dative singular form of the definite article ge and heg (“hedge”).
See also Limburgish Gerhègke.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Weert, Limburg, Netherlands
a village in Peel en Maas, Limburg, Netherlands

grashoek.e: First attested as Grashoek in 1803-1820. Compound of gras (“grass”) and hoek (“corner, isolated place”).

s.def: a hamlet in Peel en Maas, Limburg, Netherlands

groeze.e: Derived from Middle Dutch groese (“pasture, grassland”). Compare Groessen.

s.def: a village in Horst aan de Maas, Limburg, Netherlands

griendtsveen.e: First attested as de maatschappij Griendtsveen in 1887. Compound of van de Griendt (a surname) and veen (“peat bog, marsh”). Named after Joannes van de Griendt (1804-1884), an investor who developed the lands around Griendtsveen and Helenaveen.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Horst aan de Maas, Limburg, Netherlands

grubbenvorst.e: First attested as vurste in 1207. Compound of the toponym Grubben and vorst (“preserve”). The toponym Grubben refers to a former castle and is derived from dialectal grubbe (“dug passage, hollow road”). So named to distinguish the settlement from Broekhuizenvorst.

s.def: a hamlet in Horst aan de Maas, Limburg, Netherlands

genenberg.e: First attested as Genenberg in 1913. Derived from a compound of the demonstrative pronoun gene and berg (“hill, elevated place”).

s.def: a village in Venray, Limburg, Netherlands

geijsteren.e: First attested as gesserne in 1236. Compound of geest (“elevated sandy land”) and Old Dutch heri (“sandy ridge”). Compare Gasteren and Geesteren.

s.def: the unpleasant smell that arises from a grave; a sepulchral smell
a village in Land van Cuijk, North Brabant, Netherlands

groeningen.e: First attested as grunenhem in 1459. Compound of Old Dutch Gruno (a personal name) and heem (“home, settlement”). Compare Groningen.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Land van Cuijk, North Brabant, Netherlands

gassel.e: First attested as gassel in 1306. Likely a compound of Old Dutch gast (“guest”) and sale (“hall”), here referring to an inn.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Oss, North Brabant, Netherlands

geffen.e: First attested as geffene in 1246. Etymology uncertain. Potentially a toponymic derivation from Proto-West Germanic *gabulu (“fork”).

s.def: a hamlet in Oss, North Brabant, Netherlands

gement.e: First attested as Gement in 1936. Derived from a dialectal variant of gemeente (“community, municipality”), used here to denote communal lands.

s.def: a hamlet in Maashorst, North Brabant, Netherlands

graspeel.e: First attested as Graspeel in 1653. Compound of gras (“grass”) and the toponym Peel. The toponym refers to a specific section of the Peel region that was used for pasture.

s.def: a municipality of North Brabant, Netherlands
a village and former municipality of Gemert-Bakel, North Brabant, Netherlands

gemert.e: Potentially attested as gamerthe between 1076-1081, attested with certainty as gemerde in 1172. Potentially derived from a collectivised noun Proto-Germanic *mari- (“lake, cut-off riverbend”) prefixed with the collectivising prefix *ga-. Compare Gemonde.

s.def: a hamlet in Gemert-Bakel, North Brabant, Netherlands

geneneind.e: First attested as te ghenen eijnde in 1425. Compound of the definite article ge and eind (“edge, end”). So named to distinguish the settlement from Mathijseind and Nuijeneind.

s.def: a hamlet in Gemert-Bakel, North Brabant, Netherlands

grotel.e: First attested as de grotel in 1288. Compound of groot (“great, big”) and lo (“light forest”).

s.def: a hamlet in Deurne, North Brabant, Netherlands

groot bruggen.e: First attested as brugghen in 1340. Derived from groot (“big, great”) and the dative singular form of brug (“bridge”). The element groot was added to distinguish the settlement from Klein Bruggen.

s.def: a hamlet in Deurne, North Brabant, Netherlands

groote bottel.e: First attested as bothele in 1246. Derived from groot (“big, great”) and a compound of unclear derivation, perhaps composed of Middle Dutch bottel (“dogwood, Cornus sp.”) and lo (“light forest”). The element groot was added to distinguish the settlement from Kleine Bottel. Compare Budel.

s.def: a hamlet in Someren, North Brabant, Netherlands

groote hoeven.e: First attested as de Hoeven in 1838-1857. Derived from groot (“big, great”) and the plural form of hoeve (“piece of land of a certain size”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Cranendonck, North Brabant, Netherlands

gastel.e: First attested as gastele in 1307. Derived from the common toponymic element gastel (“inn, guesthouse”).

s.def: a hamlet in Heeze-Leende, North Brabant, Netherlands

ginderover.e: First attested as ghender over in 1312-1350. Compound of ginder (“yonder”) and over (“on the other side”). Compare Ginderdoor.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Valkenswaard, North Brabant, Netherlands

geenhoven.e: First attested as ghinhoeven in 1343. Compound of an inflected form of the definite article ge and the dative plural form of hof (“farmstead, homestead”).

s.def: a hamlet in Hilvarenbeek, North Brabant, Netherlands

gorp.e: First attested as gorpe and goerp in 1312-1350. Probably a compound of Middle Dutch goor (“swampy woodland”) and Old Dutch *apa (“river, stream”).

s.def: a hamlet in Hilvarenbeek, North Brabant, Netherlands

groot loo.e: Potentially attested as lo in the first quarter of the 13th century, attested with certainty as t Loo in 1566. Derived from groot (“big, great”) and lo (“light forest”). So named to distinguish the settlement from Klein Loo.

s.def: a hamlet in Hilvarenbeek, North Brabant, Netherlands

grote voort.e: Potentially attested as hervort in 1303, attested with certainty as t Voort in 1556. Derived from groot (“big, great”) and voorde (“ford, crossing”). So named to distinguish the settlement from Kleine Voort.

s.def: a former municipality of North Brabant, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Eindhoven, North Brabant, Netherlands

gerardusplein.e: Compound of Gerardus (a personal name) and plein (“square”). Named after the neighbourhood's central square.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Eindhoven, North Brabant, Netherlands

genneperzijde.e: Compound of the toponym Gennep and zijde (“side”).

s.def: a hamlet in Geldrop-Mierlo, North Brabant, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Eindhoven, North Brabant, Netherlands

gijzenrooi.e:
* (Geldrop-Mierlo) First attested as ghisenrode in 1303. Compound of Old Dutch Gîso (a personal name) and rode (“land cleared of trees”).
* (Eindhoven) Named after the hamlet in Geldrop-Mierlo.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Eindhoven, North Brabant, Netherlands

gildebuurt.e: Compound of gilde (“guild, trade”) and buurt (“neighbourhood”). The neighbourhood was named after the fact that many of its streets are named after traditional trades and industries.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Eindhoven, North Brabant, Netherlands

generalenbuurt.e: Compound of the plural form of generaal (“general”) and buurt (“neighbourhood”). The neighbourhood was named after the fact that many of its streets are named after generals.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Eindhoven, North Brabant, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Eindhoven, North Brabant, Netherlands

genderdal.e: Compound of the hydronym Gender and dal (“dale, valley”). Compare Genderen.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Eindhoven, North Brabant, Netherlands

genderbeemd.e: First attested as De Beemden in 1897. Compound of the hydronym Gender and beemd (“alluvial land, pasture”). Compare Genderen.

s.def: a municipality of North Brabant, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Geldrop-Mierlo, North Brabant, Netherlands

genoenhuis.e: First attested as Genaanhuis in 1838-1857. If the attestation of 1838 is correct, the toponym is a contracted compound of the definite article ge, oud (“old”) and huis (“house”). If the 1838 attestation is a scribal error, then the toponym is a contracted compound of the definite article ge, nieu...

s.def: a hamlet in Helmond, North Brabant, Netherlands

geeneind.e: First attested as gheeneynde in 1452. Compound of the definite article ge and eind (“edge, end”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Helmond, North Brabant, Netherlands

ganzenwinkel.e: First attested as Ganzenwinkel in 1866. Compound of the plural form of gans (“goose, Anser sp.”) and winkel (“corner, enclosed piece of land”).

s.def: a hamlet in Alphen-Chaam, North Brabant, Netherlands
a hamlet in Laarbeek, North Brabant, Netherlands

ginderdoor.e:
* (Alphen-Chaam) First attested as ghinderen doer in 1528. Compound of ginder (“yonder”) and door (“through, past”). Compare Ginderover.
* (Laarbeek) First attested as ginderdore in 1311. Compound of ginder (“yonder”) and door (“through, past”). Compare Ginderover.

s.def: a village in Sint-Michielsgestel, North Brabant, Netherlands

gemonde.e: First attested as datmunda in 698-699. Derived from Old Dutch munt (“mouth of a river”) prefixed with the collectivising prefix ga-. Compare Gemert.

s.def: a hamlet in Heusden, North Brabant, Netherlands

giersbergen.e: First attested as ghiersberch in 1312. Compound of an uncertain first element and berg (“hill, elevated place”). The first element may be the personal name Middle Dutch Gheer.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Altena, North Brabant, Netherlands

genderen.e: First attested as ganderon in the late 11th century. Derived from an ancient hydronym. The hydronym has cognates in a large area of Western Europe and may be derived from Proto-Germanic *gandu (“opening, gap”). An alternative hypothesis proposes a derivation from Pre-Indo-European *gand- "gravel"...

s.def: a village and former municipality of Altena, North Brabant, Netherlands

giessen.e: First attested as giscen in 1178. Etymology uncertain. May be derived from a hydronym related to Proto-Germanic *geusaną (“flow powerfully, pour”). Compare Giessendam.

s.def: a hamlet in Drimmelen, North Brabant, Netherlands

gaete.e: First attested as de Gaete in 1846. Derived from the dative singular form of gat (“mouth of a stream”). Compare Gieten.

s.def: a municipality of North Brabant, Netherlands
a village in Gilze en Rijen, North Brabant, Netherlands

gilze.e: First attested as gilisa in 1012-1018. Perhaps derived from an ancient hydronym related to Old Norse gil (“gap, ravine”). An alternative hypothesis proposes a compound derived from Old Dutch *gîsil (“twig, branch”) and lo (“light forest”), but this proposed etymology does not match well with the ...

s.def: a city and municipality of North Brabant, Netherlands

geertruidenberg.e: First attested as apud Montem Sancte Gertrudis in 1213. Compound of Geertruid (a personal name) and berg (“hill, elevated place”). Named after Saint Gertrude of Nivelles.

s.def: a village and municipality of North Brabant, Netherlands

goirle.e: First attested as gorle in 1215. Compound of goor (“swampy forest soil”) and lo (“light forest”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Goirle, North Brabant, Netherlands

grobbendonck.e: Named after the Counts of Grobbendonk.

s.def: a hamlet in Baarle-Nassau, North Brabant, Netherlands

gorpeind.e: First attested as Gorps hoff in 1699. Compound of van Gorp (a surname) and eind (“end, edge”).

s.def: a hamlet in Baarle-Nassau, North Brabant, Netherlands

groot-bedaf.e: First attested as bodaef and boudaf in 1312-1350. Compound of groot (“big, greater”) and a toponym of unknown origin. The element groot was added to distinguish the settlement from Klein-Bedaf. See also Bedaf.

s.def: a village in Alphen-Chaam, North Brabant, Netherlands

galder.e: First attested as galre in 1299. Potentially derived from a hydronym derived from Proto-Germanic *galan (“sing, rage”) or gal (“bile, yellow”). Compare Gaal and Middegaal.

s.def: a hamlet in Alphen-Chaam, North Brabant, Netherlands

geersbroek.e: First attested as t ghaersbroeck in 1451. Compound of Middle Dutch gaers (“grass, grassland”) and broek (“marsh, marshland”).

s.def: a hamlet in Alphen-Chaam, North Brabant, Netherlands

goedentijd.e: First attested as aenden goeden tyt in 1542. Compound of an inflected form of goed (“good”) and tijd (“time”). Originally the name of a field.

s.def: a neighbourhood and former municipality of Breda, North Brabant, Netherlands

ginneken.e: First attested as ghinneken in 1246. Etymology unknown. Tentatively linked to the German toponym Ginnick. Potentially derived from Old Dutch Ginno (a personal name) if the link to Ginnick is valid. Formerly an independent village.

s.def: a former municipality of North Brabant, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Breda, North Brabant, Netherlands

geeren.e: First attested as de geeren in 1579. Derived from the plural form of geer (“pointed piece of land”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Bergen op Zoom, North Brabant, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Breda, North Brabant, Netherlands

gageldonk.e:
* (Bergen op Zoom) Compound of gagel (“bog-myrtle, Myrica gale”) and donk (“sandy hill protruding above a swamp”).
* (Breda) First attested as gageldunc in 1211-1212. Compound of gagel (“bog-myrtle, Myrica gale”) and donk (“sandy hill protruding above a swamp”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Etten-Leur, North Brabant, Netherlands

grauwe polder.e: First attested as Graauwe Polder in 1838-1857. Derived from grauw (“bleak, grey”) and polder (“polder”).

s.def: a hamlet in Halderberge, North Brabant, Netherlands

galgestraat.e: First attested as Galgestraat in 1865. Compound of galg (“gallows”) and straat (“street, way”). Named after a street that led to the gallows of the Hoeven court of law.

s.def: a hamlet in Halderberge, North Brabant, Netherlands

gastelsveer.e: First attested as Gastelsveer in 1865. Compound of the toponym Oud Gastel and veer (“ferry, crossing”).

s.def: a hamlet in Tholen, Zeeland, Netherlands
a village in Noord-Beveland, Zeeland, Netherlands

geersdijk.e:
First attested as gerolsdike in 1216. Compound of Middle Dutch Gerolf (a personal name) and dijk (“levee”).
See also Zealandic Heersdiek.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Veere, Zeeland, Netherlands

gapinge.e:
First attested as gapinge in 1216. Derived from Old Dutch *gapa (“gap, opening”) suffixed with -ingô-.
See also Zealandic Gaepienge, Ter Gaepienge.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Veere, Zeeland, Netherlands

grijpskerke.e:
First attested as gripeskirca in 1181-1210. Compound of Old Dutch Gripe (a personal name) and kirca (“church”).
See also Zealandic Grieps, Hrieps.

s.def: a hamlet in Veere, Zeeland, Netherlands

geldtienden.e: Attested as Geldtienden in 1936. Compound of either Middle Dutch gelte (“infertile female pig”) or geld (“money”) and the plural form of tiende (“church tithe”).

s.def: a hamlet in Veere, Zeeland, Netherlands

groot-valkenisse.e:
First attested as Valkenisse in the 18th century. Compound of groot (“big, great”) and a compound toponym derived from Middle Dutch Valke (a personal name) and nisse (“headland, spit”). The element groot was added to distinguish the settlement from Klein-Valkenisse.
See also Zealandic Groôt Valke...

s.def: a former municipality of Zeeland, Netherlands
a hamlet in Vlissingen, Zeeland, Netherlands

groot-abeele.e:
First attested as abeel in 1370. Compound of groot (“big, great”) and Middle Dutch abeel (“silver poplar, Populus alba”). The element groot was added to distinguish the settlement from Nieuw-Abeele.
See also Zealandic Groôt-Abeêle.

s.def: a hamlet in Borsele, Zeeland, Netherlands

graszode.e:
Attested as de Grasvazen in 1838-1857. Derived from graszode (“sod of grass”).
See also Zealandic De Hosvazze, De Gosvazze.

s.def: a hamlet in Goes, Zeeland, Netherlands

goese sas.e:
Attested as SAS-VAN-GOES (HET) of het Goessche-Sas in 1847. Derived from the toponym Goes and sas (“lift-lock”).
See also Zealandic 't Sas.

s.def: a hamlet in Reimerswaal, Zeeland, Netherlands

gawege.e:
First attested as van den damme van de ghawege in 1288. Derived from Middle Dutch ghawege (“connecting road”). Compare Delfgauw, Gaag and Gauw.
See also Zealandic Gaewege.

s.def: a village in Hulst, Zeeland, Netherlands

graauw.e: First attested as grotha in 1170. Derived from Old Dutch grode (“overgrown alluvial land deposited by the sea”). Compare De Groe, Groede and Groet.

s.def: a former municipality of Zeeland, Netherlands

graauw en langendam.e: Named after Graauw and the Langendam polder.

s.def: a hamlet in Terneuzen, Zeeland, Netherlands

griete.e: Attested as Griete (De) in 1899. Derived from a shortened form of Margaretha (a personal name). The settlement was named for its location in the Margarethapolder, which was named in turn after its chief investor, Margaretha de Mauregnault. Compare Magrette and Margret.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Sluis, Zeeland, Netherlands

groede.e:
First attested as groede in 1133. Derived from Old Dutch grode (“overgrown alluvial land deposited by the sea”). Compare Graauw, De Groe and Groet.
See also Zealandic De Groe.

s.def: a city and former municipality of Kampen, Overijssel, Netherlands

grafhorst.e:
First attested as grafhorst in 1277. Compound of Middle Dutch graf (“grave”) and horst (“overgrown elevated place”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Graffest.

s.def: a city and former municipality of Zwartewaterland, Overijssel, Netherlands

genemuiden.e:
First attested as genemuden in 1275. Compound of the hydronym Genne (derived from Proto-Germanic *gan- (“bright, shining”)) and the dative plural form of Old Dutch *mūtha (“mouth”), an Ingvaeonic variant of munt (cf. Old Saxon, Old Frisian mūth). Compare Genne and Gennep.
See also Dutch Low Saxon...

s.def: a hamlet in Zwartewaterland, Overijssel, Netherlands

genne.e: First attested as ghenne in 1348. Derived from an ancient hydronym, derived in turn from Proto-Germanic *gan- (“bright, shining”). Compare Genemuiden and Gennep.

s.def: a hamlet in Zwartewaterland, Overijssel, Netherlands

genne-overwaters.e: First attested as de Werf in 1830-1855. Compound of the toponym Genne and a compound of over (“across”) and water (“water”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Steenwijkerland, Overijssel, Netherlands

giethoorn.e: First attested as prope gethorne in 1232-1233. Compound of an unclear first element and Middle Dutch horn (“protruding piece of land, corner”). The first element may be Middle Dutch geit (“wild female deer”).

examples: Elk van de onderstaande zinnen schendt een geslaagdheidsvoorwaarde van de taalhandeling beloven.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Zwolle, Overijssel, Netherlands

gerenbroek.e: Compound of the toponym Geren and broek (“marsh, marshland”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Zwolle, Overijssel, Netherlands

gerenlanden.e: Compound of the toponym Geren and the plural form of land (“land”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Dalfsen, Overijssel, Netherlands

gerner marke.e: Compound of the toponym Gerner and mark (“undivided communal land”).

s.def: a hamlet in Ommen, Overijssel, Netherlands

giethmen.e: First attested as gheetmen, gheetmunde and gheetmende in 1381-1383. Etymology unknown. Compare Bathmen, Berkum, Besthmen, Diermen, Fortmond, Helpman, Hertme, Ratum, Reutum, Thamen, Wachtum and Wijthmen.

s.def: a city and former municipality of Hardenberg, Overijssel, Netherlands

gramsbergen.e:
First attested as bergene in 1227. Compound of Old Dutch Gram (a personal name) and berg (“hill, elevated place”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Gramsbargen, Grambarge.

s.def: a hamlet in Hardenberg, Overijssel, Netherlands

gouden ploeg.e: First attested as Gouden ploeg in 1899. Derived from gouden (“golden”) and ploeg (“plough”). Named after a farmhouse. The toponym refers to the fertility of the soil around the settlement.

s.def: a hamlet in Hardenberg, Overijssel, Netherlands

groot-oever.e: First attested as honouere in 1283. Compound of groot (“great, big”) and oever (“riverside”). The settlement was named for its location on the banks of the Reest. The element groot was added to distinguish the settlement from Lutten-Oever.

s.def: a village in Twenterand, Overijssel, Netherlands

geerdijk.e: First attested as Geerdijk in 1913. Compound of geer (“wedge-shaped, tapering”) and dijk (“levee”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Deventer, Overijssel, Netherlands

groot douwel.e: First attested as van dovelt in 1284. Derived from groot (“great, big”) and a compound of Middle Dutch dauw (“dew”) and veld (“field”). The element groot was added to distinguish the settlement from Klein Douwel.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Deventer, Overijssel, Netherlands

graveland.e: Compound of the toponym Grave and land (“land”). Named after the former Grave farmstead.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Hof van Twente, Overijssel, Netherlands

gijmink.e: First attested as ghyminc and ghiminc in 1333. Derived from Old Dutch Gimo (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ing-.

s.def: a hamlet in Dinkelland, Overijssel, Netherlands

gammelke.e: First attested as gamminclo in 1272. Compound of Old Dutch Gammo (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ing- and lo (“light forest”).

s.def: a hamlet in Dinkelland, Overijssel, Netherlands

groot agelo.e: First attested as de agelo in 1252. Derived from groot (“great, big”) and a compound of Old Dutch Ago (a personal name) and lo (“light forest”). The element groot was added to distinguish the settlement from Klein Agelo.

s.def: a village in Losser, Overijssel, Netherlands

glane.e: First attested as De Glaaner beek in 1773-1794. Derived from a hydronym, which is in turn cognate to Old Norse glana (“clear”) and glanzen (“shine, sparkle”). Compare Geleen and Glanerbrug.

s.def: a hamlet in Losser, Overijssel, Netherlands

glane-beekhoek.e: Compound of the toponyms Glane and Beekhoek (compound of beek (“brook, stream”) and hoek (“isolated place, sector”)). Beekhoek was originally the name of a field.

s.def: a village in Enschede, Overijssel, Netherlands

glanerbrug.e: First attested as Glanenbrug in 1665. Compound of the hydronym Glane (cognate with Old Norse glana (“clear”) and glanzen (“shine, sparkle”)) and brug (“bridge”). Compare Glane.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Oldenzaal, Overijssel, Netherlands

graven es.e: First attested as Bentheimer Esch in 1830-1855. Derived from the plural form of graaf (“count”) and es (“farmland next to a village”). The element graaf refers to the Counts of Bentheim.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Enschede, Overijssel, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Enschede, Overijssel, Netherlands

glanerveld.e: Compound of the toponym Glane and veld (“field”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Enschede, Overijssel, Netherlands

glanermaten.e: Compound of the toponym Glane and the plural form of a variant of made (“pasture”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Hengelo, Overijssel, Netherlands

groot driene.e: First attested as Groot Driene in 1830-1855. Derived from groot (“great, big”) and the toponym Driene. The element groot was added to distinguish the settlement from Klein Driene.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Kampen, Overijssel, Netherlands

greente.e: First attested as haegheningher grynt in 1364. Derived from a variant of griend (“land next to water used to cultivate osiers”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Goes, Zeeland, Netherlands

goese meer.e: Derived from the toponym Goes and meer (“lake”). Named after a nearby lake.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Goes, Zeeland, Netherlands

goese polder.e: Derived from the toponym Goes and polder (“polder”). Named after a polder.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Hulst, Zeeland, Netherlands

groote kreek.e: Attested as Groote kreek in 1866. Derived from groot (“great, big”) and kreek (“creek”). Originally a hydronym.

gildewijk.e: Both compounds of gilde (“guild”) and wijk (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhoods' streets are named after various guilds.

a village in Westerveld, Drenthe, Netherlands

geeuwenbrug.e:
First attested as die Crumme voerdt offt guijwe in 1617-1618. Compound of dialectal gouwe (“word designating a watercourse in peat bog areas”) and brug (“bridge”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Geeuwenbrugge.

gijsselte.e:
Potentially attested as gislo in 1141, attested with certainty as in gisle in 1217. Compound of Proto-Germanic *gîsil (“young branch, twig”) and lo (“light forest”). The current ending on -elte developed under the influence of nearby toponyms.

garminge.e:
First attested as gharminge and gherminge in 1362. Derived from Old Dutch Garm (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ing-.
See also Dutch Low Saxon Garming.

gees.e:
Potentially attested as gies in 1207 or 1208, attested with certainty as ghies in 1217. Etymology unknown. Potentially related to Proto-Germanic *gais (“infertile”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Gies.

geesbrug.e:
First attested as Geesbrug in 1913. Compound of the toponym Gees and brug (“bridge”).

grevenberg.e: First attested as GREVENBERG in 1843. Compound of the plural form of graf (“grave”) and berg (“hill, elevated place”). The settlement was constructed near a prehistoric tumulus.

gasselte.e:
Potentially attested as gesholte in 1302, attested with certainty as gasselte in 1359. Etymology uncertain. It is unknown whether the oldest attestation refers to the current settlement or to a forest near Gees.

gasselternijveen.e:
First attested as Nieuw Gasselter Veen in 1739. Compound of the toponym Gasselte, nij (“new”) and veen (“peat bog, marsh”).

gasselternijveenschemond.e:
First attested as Gasselter-Nijeveenstermond in 1843. Compound of the toponym Gasselternijveen and mond (“small canal that links up with a primary canal”).

gasteren.e:
First attested as in villa ghestre and apud ghesteren in 1298-1304. Potentially derived from a compound of geest (“elevated sandy land”) and Old Dutch *wari (“inhabitants”) or heri (“sandy ridge”). Compare Geesteren and Geijsteren.

gieterveen.e:
First attested as gheter vene in 1458. Compound of the toponym Gieten and veen (“peat bog, marsh”).

grolloo.e:
First attested as grunlo in the 12th century. Compound of Old Dutch grôn (“green”) and lo (“light forest”). Compare Groenlo and Rolde.

gasselterboerveen.e: First attested as Boerveen in 1831. Compound of the toponym Gasselte and a compound of boer (“farmer”) and veen (“peat bog, marsh”).

gasselterboerveenschemond.e:
First attested as GASSELTER-BOERVEENSTERMOND in 1843. Compound of the toponym Gasselterboerveen and mond (“small canal that links up with a primary canal”).

geelbroek.e: First attested as dat gelebroeck in 1444 and 1447. Compound of geel (“yellow”) and broek (“marsh, marshland”). The element geel may refer to vegetation such as bog myrtle and broom.

gieterzandvoort.e:
First attested as Zandvoort of Gieterzandvoort in 1913. Compound of the toponym Gieten and a compound of zand (“sand”) and voort (“ford, crossing”).

graswijk.e:
First attested as Graswijk in 1851-1855. Compound of gras (“grass”) and wijk (“farmstead, settlement, village”).

garmerwolde.e: First attested as germerwolt and germerwalda in the 13th century. Compound of Old Frisian Geldmer (a personal name) and wald (“marsh forest, swamp forest”).

gorechtbuurt.e: Compound of the toponym Gorecht and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood is named after one of the former administrative districts of the Ommelanden.

gravenburg.e: Attested as Gravenburg in 1983. Named after a farmhouse.

gaarland.e: First attested as Gaarland in 1867. Compound of a variant of geer (“wedge-shaped piece of land”) and land (“land”).

ganzedijk.e: First attested as GANZEDIJK in 1843. Compound of gans (“goose, Anser sp.”) and dijk (“levee”).

garsthuizen.e: First attested as gershusen in the 13th century. Compound of Old Frisian gers (“grass”) and the dative plural form of hûs (“house”). The first element was gradually reinterpreted to Dutch Low Saxon gaarst (“barley”).

geefsweer.e: First attested as gewesweer in 1306. Compound of Old Frisian *Gevi (a personal name) and weer (“artificial mound used for habitation”).

garreweer.e: Potentially attested as GEROEIEVVERE in 1068-1090, attested with certainty as garreaweere in 1325. Compound of an unclear first element and Old Frisian weer (“artificial mound used for habitation”).

Derived from groot (“big, great”) and the toponym Wetsinge. The toponym contrasts with Klein Wetsinge.

garnwerd.e: First attested as granauurð in the 10th or 11th century. Compound of an unclear first element and Old Frisian uurð (“artificial mound used for habitation”). The first element may be Old Frisian Grano (a personal name).

giekerk.e: First attested as ghetzercka in 1242. Borrowed from West Frisian Gytsjerk, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian Gye (a personal name) and tzercka (“church”).

goudriaan.e: First attested as goudriaen in 1253. Derived from the hydronym Goudriaan, of unknown etymology.

groot-ammers.e: First attested as ammers in 1280-1287. Compound of groot (“great, big”) and a hydronym potentially derived from a form reconstructed as *Ambarussa.
